Basel

BSBasel-Stadt

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The Basilisk's mirror

After the devastating 1356 earthquake, a basilisk emerges from the ruins and haunts medieval Basel with its deadly gaze. A brave child must find a mirror somewhere in the shattered city to show the creature its own reflection -- the only way to break its power and allow Basel to rebuild.

Create This Story

The Basilisk -- a creature with the head and claws of a rooster, the body of a dragon, and the tail of a serpent -- is Basel's heraldic animal and guardian symbol. According to legend, a basilisk could kill with a single glance; the only way to defeat it was to show it its own reflection. On 18 October 1356, a catastrophic earthquake struck Basel, the most significant seismological event in Central European recorded history, nearly levelling the city. In 1884, artist Wilhelm Bubeck designed the Basilisk fountain, manufactured 50 times and placed throughout the city.

Morgenstreich magic

At 4:00 AM on Fasnacht Monday, the lights go out and a child's lantern begins to glow with a life of its own. The enchanted lantern pulls them through 500 years of carnival history in one magical night -- from the first torch-lit Morgenstreich of 1835 to the modern parade of 11,000 costumed participants.

Create This Story

The Basler Fasnacht is the largest carnival in Switzerland and the biggest Protestant carnival in the world, inscribed on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage List in 2017. At precisely 4:00 AM on Monday, all streetlights are extinguished and the city plunges into darkness. The only light comes from the Laternen of the Cliquen -- carnival groups marching with Piccolo flutes and Basel drums. The first officially permitted Morgenstreich was held in 1835 with burning torches; pole-mounted lanterns appeared in 1845.

The Wickelfisch voyage

A child's Wickelfisch swimming bag falls into the Rhine and floats downstream. Chasing it by ferry and bridge, the child discovers the stories of each crossing -- meeting the Wild Maa, the Leu, and the Vogel Gryff along the way, and learning why Basel's two halves need the river between them.

Create This Story

Swimming in the Rhine is a beloved Basel summer tradition dating back centuries -- nuns from the Klingental abbey swam the Rhine as early as the 15th century. The annual Basler Rheinschwimmen has been held since 1980, drawing up to 4,000 swimmers each August. Swimmers carry belongings in the iconic Wickelfisch -- a waterproof, fish-shaped inflatable bag invented in Basel. Four traditional cable ferries still cross the Rhine: the Wild Maa, Leu, Vogel Gryff, and Ueli, all propelled solely by the river current.

Erasmus and the lost manuscript

A page escapes from Froben's printing press and blows through Basel's streets in the wind. A child and the old scholar Erasmus must chase it past the Muenster, the Rathaus, and the university before the ink fades forever -- a race through the city that made books change the world.

Create This Story

The great humanist Erasmus of Rotterdam arrived in Basel in 1514 to have his works printed by Johann Froben. In 1516, Froben published Erasmus's landmark Novum Testamentum -- a critical Greek New Testament that Martin Luther used for his German Bible translation. Froben employed Hans Holbein the Younger as an illustrator and controlled up to seven presses. Erasmus died in Basel in 1536 and was buried in the Protestant Muenster despite being a Catholic clergyman.

The rooster's trial

Basel, 1474: a rooster is accused of laying a basilisk egg and faces execution. A child befriends the doomed bird and must prove its innocence before the city council at the Rathaus -- navigating medieval superstition, courtroom drama, and the fine line between legend and truth.

Create This Story

In 1474, a rooster in Basel was sentenced to death and publicly burned because it was accused of having laid an egg -- citizens feared the egg would hatch into a basilisk. The basilisk, Basel's heraldic creature, could supposedly kill with a single glance. The name 'Basel' and 'Basilisk' share an etymological connection from the Greek 'basileus' meaning king. This bizarre historical event reflects medieval superstition about the city's legendary creature.

Euler's impossible problem

Young Leonhard Euler discovers that the bridges of Basel hold a mathematical secret. A child follows his chalk equations across the Mittlere Bruecke, through Kleinbasel, and back -- solving an impossible puzzle that proves mathematics can be found in every stone and stream of the city.

Create This Story

Leonhard Euler (1707-1783), born in Basel and educated at the University of Basel under Johann Bernoulli, was one of the most prolific mathematicians in history with over 800 research papers. He introduced the symbols e (Euler's number), pi in its modern usage, and the summation notation to mathematics. The University of Basel, founded on 4 April 1460, is Switzerland's oldest university. The Mittlere Bruecke, opened in 1226, is one of the oldest Rhine crossings.

The red stones remember

A child touches the red sandstone of the Muenster and is pulled back to 18 October 1356. They must escape the earthquake -- the firestorms, the collapsing towers -- and help the citizens of Basel begin to rebuild their cathedral, one red stone at a time, from rubble into the landmark that stands today.

Create This Story

Basel Muenster is built from distinctive red Buntsandstein (red sandstone) with a roof of colourful patterned tiles. The original was consecrated in 1019 under Emperor Heinrich II. On 18 October 1356, the most devastating earthquake in Central European history struck Basel, destroying all churches and towers. The Muenster lost five of its towers. The current structure blends Romanesque and Gothic styles from the post-earthquake reconstruction. Its two asymmetrical towers -- the Georgsturm and the Martinsturm -- define Basel's skyline.

The Vogel Gryff's dance

The three legendary figures of Kleinbasel -- Wild Man, Griffin, and Lion -- come alive from the ferry boats but have forgotten their traditional dance steps. A child must help each one remember before the festival begins, crossing back and forth over the Mittlere Bruecke while keeping their backs firmly turned to Grossbasel.

Create This Story

The Vogel Gryff festival is Kleinbasel's proudest tradition, rooted in the 14th century and celebrated continuously since the 16th century. Three legendary figures -- the Wilde Maa (Wild Man), the Vogel Gryff (Griffin), and the Leu (Lion) -- represent Kleinbasel's three historic guilds. The figures perform traditional dances on the Mittlere Bruecke, famously keeping their backs turned to Grossbasel -- a playful gesture of the centuries-old rivalry between Basel's two halves.
